summary: P/Invoke wrapper methods throughout SkiaSharp do not call GC.KeepAlive on reference-type parameters after native calls, allowing the GC to prematurely collect managed objects (and finalize their native handles) while native code is still operating on them — a race condition that can cause use-after-free crashes.

bugSignals.errorMessage: Premature GC collection of managed objects during P/Invoke — use-after-free race condition

stepsToReproduce: Call a SkiaSharp method that passes managed object handles to P/Invoke (e.g., canvas.DrawPicture(picture))
Ensure the managed object is not referenced after the call site
Under GC pressure, the managed object may be collected during the P/Invoke call
The finalizer closes the native handle while native code is still using it

repoLinks.description: Uno Platform PR showing real-world Skia-related GC collection issue with DrawPicture
dotnet/java-interop issue demonstrating the same GC/finalizer race pattern
Chris Brumme blog post explaining the GC lifetime/KeepAlive/handle recycling problem

versionAnalysis.relevanceReason: Only 14 GC.KeepAlive calls exist across 910+ P/Invoke call sites. The vast majority of wrapper methods remain unprotected.

analysis.summary: The .NET GC can collect managed objects once they are no longer referenced in managed code, even during an instance method call. When SkiaSharp extracts a native handle (via .Handle) and passes it to a P/Invoke, the owning managed object becomes eligible for collection. If the finalizer runs and disposes the native handle while the P/Invoke is still executing, use-after-free occurs. PR #1258 (2020) added GC.KeepAlive in a handful of cases (14 calls total), but the vast majority of the 910+ P/Invoke call sites remain unprotected. PR #3394 is already open to address this systematically.
analysis.rationale: This is a well-documented .NET runtime behavior confirmed by Chris Brumme's blog post and demonstrated in real-world code (Uno Platform PR #21660). The reporter (jonpryor) is a .NET runtime expert. The issue is clearly a bug — the current code has a race condition between the application and GC/finalizer threads. The scope is large (910+ P/Invoke calls, only 14 protected) making this a systematic codebase-wide issue, not a one-off fix.

keySignals.text: Once we've extracted _handle from this, there are no further uses of this object. In other words, this can be collected even while you are executing an instance method on that object.
keySignals.interpretation: Authoritative explanation of the GC race condition from a .NET runtime engineer.

keySignals.text: meaning once execution hits SkiaApi.sk_canvas_draw_picture(), nothing ensures that picture is kept alive. (Or canvas, for that matter!)
keySignals.interpretation: Reporter correctly identifies both the parameter and this as vulnerable to premature collection.

keySignals.text: This should be done for all public methods that invoke P/Invoke methods.
keySignals.interpretation: Reporter requests a systematic audit, not just a point fix. Confirmed by the 14-vs-910 ratio.

codeInvestigation.finding: DrawPicture passes picture.Handle and paint.Handle to sk_canvas_draw_picture but has no GC.KeepAlive for picture, paint, or this — exactly as reported.
ReadPixels has GC.KeepAlive(this) after its P/Invoke call — one of only 14 protected call sites. Confirms the pattern exists but was applied inconsistently.
SKData has 5 GC.KeepAlive calls protecting stream parameters in Create methods — shows the fix pattern is already established in the codebase.
DrawPicture overload with matrix also lacks GC.KeepAlive. SKCanvas has 67 P/Invoke calls total, none protected.

workarounds: Callers can add GC.KeepAlive(obj) after any SkiaSharp method call where the object might not be referenced afterward to prevent premature collection.
nextQuestions: Should GC.KeepAlive(this) be added to all instance methods, or only where the caller might not hold a reference?
Can the code generator (utils/generate.ps1) be extended to automatically emit GC.KeepAlive for P/Invoke wrapper methods?
Is PR #3394 sufficient in scope, or does it need to cover all 910+ call sites?

resolution.hypothesis: The .NET GC can collect managed objects once their handle is extracted for P/Invoke, before the native call returns. GC.KeepAlive must be called after every P/Invoke that uses handles from managed parameters.

proposals.title: Add GC.KeepAlive to all P/Invoke wrapper methods
proposals.description: Systematically add GC.KeepAlive calls for all reference-type parameters (including this) after every P/Invoke call in the binding layer. PR #3394 is already open with an initial implementation.

proposals.title: Caller-side GC.KeepAlive workaround
proposals.description: Callers can add GC.KeepAlive(picture) after calls like canvas.DrawPicture(picture) to prevent premature collection until the fix is merged.

proposals.title: Extend code generator to emit GC.KeepAlive automatically
proposals.description: Modify utils/generate.ps1 to automatically emit GC.KeepAlive calls in generated P/Invoke wrappers, preventing future regressions as new APIs are added.

resolution.recommendedProposal: Add GC.KeepAlive to all P/Invoke wrapper methods
resolution.recommendedReason: PR #3394 is already open with an initial implementation. The systematic approach is necessary given the 910+ call sites. Caller-side workaround helps in the interim.

actionability.reason: Legitimate GC race condition confirmed by code investigation. PR #3394 is open but needs review for completeness given 910+ P/Invoke call sites. The scope and correctness of the fix needs investigation.

actions.description: Labels are already correct: type/bug, area/SkiaSharp, os/Android, tenet/reliability
Link to PR #3394 which addresses this issue
Acknowledge the bug, confirm the scope, and note the existing PR
